coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence monitoring of microRNA via Triton X-100 functionalized carbon dots-based nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yuqin
Zhang, Gui
Song, Li
Kuang, Guangrong
Ding, Yilan
Bu, Shuchun
Chai, Yaqin
Fu, Yingzi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical performance have attracted tremendous interest for the construction of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensors. However, the intrinsic self-aggregation behavior of CDs brings a serious issue of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which can quench the ECL emission. In this work, different surfactants (non-ionic, anionic and cationic) were used to modulate the preparation of functionalized CDs (CDs-based nanocomposites). Their ECL performances were significantly enhanced in potassium persulfate solution. In particular, the highest ECL intensity obtained from the Triton X-100 functionalized CDs (T-CDs) was 16.8-fold than that of CDs as ACQ was suppressed effectively. Therefore, utilizing T-CDs as an ECL luminophore, integrating with the catalytic hairpin assembly and strand displacement reaction signal amplification strategies, a "Signal on" ECL biosensor was constructed for microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) monitoring. The proposed biosensor shows a dynamic range from10 fmol·L−1 to 10 nmol·L−1 at a low detection limit of 2.9 fmol·L−1 (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, it exhibits good results for analyzing miRNA-21 in healthy human samples and the expression in MCF-7 and HeLa cells. This work not only expands the method for suppressing ACQ of CDs, but also shows great potential for the analysis of other biomarkers by replacing the corresponding DNA sequences, so as to realize the early diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Functionalized carbon dots
Aggregation-caused quenching
MicroRNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and different CDs-based nanocomposites
Further discussion on the properties of T-CDs
Feasibility verification of the biosensing strategy
Application of the biosensor in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22077105 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs (inset: HRTEM), (B) S-CDs, (C) C-CDs and (D) T-CDs. (E) XPS patterns of the full region of (a) S-CDs, (b) C-CDs and (c) T-CDs. (F) FTIR spectra of (a) CDs, (b) S-CDs, (c) C-CDs and (d) T-CDs.
Static contact angle of (A) CDs, (B) S-CDs, (C) C-CDs and (D) T-CDs. (E) HOMO and LUMO energy levels of different materials.

(A) Comparison of ECL intensity of (a) bare GCE, (b) CDs/GCE, (c) S-CDs/GCE, (d) C-CDs/GCE and (e) T-CDs/GCE (in 50 mmol·L−1 K2S2O8 solution, pH 7.4). Stability of the ECL intensities of (B) S-CDs/GCE, (C) C-CDs/GCE and (D) T-CDs/GCE.

(A) 3D ECL spectrum of T-CDs/K2S2O8 system. (B) (a) FL emission spectrum (Ex = 450 nm) and (b) 2D ECL spectrum of T-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of T-CDs (a) before and (b) after reduction. (D) EPR spectra of (a) K2S2O8 and DMPO and (b) T-CDs, K2S2O8 and DMPO. (E) FL decay curve of T-CDs.

(A) The PAGE characterization of the interaction between different samples: Lane 1, H1; Lane 2, H2; Lane 3, H1 + H2; Lane 4, miRNA-21 + H1 + H2; Lane 5, S1; Lane 6, S2; Lane 7, S1 + S2; Lane 8, miRNA-21 + H1 + H2 + S1 + S2; Lane 9, miRNA-21. (B) CV characterization for the electrode modified process in 5.0 mmol·L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- (0.1 mol·L−1 PBS, pH 7.4) at the scanning rate of 100 mV/s containing: (a) bare GCE, (b) T-CDs/GCE, (c) S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE, (d) BSA/S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE, (e) H1-H2/BSA/S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE.

ECL intensities of miRNA-21 at different concentrations on (A) T-CDs/S2O8 2− and (B) C-CDs/S2O8 2−: (a) 10 fmol·L−1, (b) 100 fmol·L−1, (c) 1 pmol·L−1, (d) 10 pmol·L−1, (e) 100 pmol·L−1, (f) 1 nmol·L−1, (g) 10 nmol·L−1. Calibration curves of miRNA-21 monitoring: (C) T-CDs/S2O8 2− and (D) C-CDs/S2O8 2− system (Error bars: SD, n = 3).

(A) Stability of the proposed biosensor. (B) Reproducibility and (C) Selectivity: (a) blank, (b) miRNA-4652–5p, (c) miRNA-24–3p, (d) miRNA-146a-5p, (e) miRNA-12, (f) mixture. (D) Biosensor detection of miRNA-21 in extracts from different numbers of MCF-7 and HeLa cells (Error bars: SD, n = 3).

(A) The formation process of T-CDs and the photographs of CDs and T-CDs powders under ultraviolet (365 nm) irradiation. (B) ECL sensing strategy for miRNA-21 monitoring. (C) Possible ECL mechanism of T-CDs in 50 mmol·L−1 K2S2O8 (0.1 mol·L−1 PBS, pH 7.4).

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive electrochemiluminescence monitoring of microRNA via Triton X-100 functionalized carbon dots-based nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical performance have attracted tremendous interest for the construction of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensors. However, the intrinsic self-aggregation behavior of CDs brings a serious issue of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which can quench the ECL emission. In this work, different surfactants (non-ionic, anionic and cationic) were used to modulate the preparation of functionalized CDs (CDs-based nanocomposites). Their ECL performances were significantly enhanced in potassium persulfate solution. In particular, the highest ECL intensity obtained from the Triton X-100 functionalized CDs (T-CDs) was 16.8-fold than that of CDs as ACQ was suppressed effectively. Therefore, utilizing T-CDs as an ECL luminophore, integrating with the catalytic hairpin assembly and strand displacement reaction signal amplification strategies, a "Signal on" ECL biosensor was constructed for microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) monitoring. The proposed biosensor shows a dynamic range from10 fmol·L−1 to 10 nmol·L−1 at a low detection limit of 2.9 fmol·L−1 (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, it exhibits good results for analyzing miRNA-21 in healthy human samples and the expression in MCF-7 and HeLa cells. This work not only expands the method for suppressing ACQ of CDs, but also shows great potential for the analysis of other biomarkers by replacing the corresponding DNA sequences, so as to realize the early diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are a class of non-protein-coding endogenous short single-stranded RNAs, and the dysfunction of miRNA has been implicated in the emergence and evolution of diseases [1]. Many studies have shown that the disorder expression of miRNAs is involved in the occurrence and development of tumors, such as lung, pancreatic, breast, cervical, prostate and other cancers [2–4]. Thus, miRNAs have been acknowledged as the promising biomarker for the diagnosis of various cancers. However, owing to the sequence homology inherent and low abundance, it is of great significance to explore the effective, rapid, accurate and sensitive sensing approaches for miRNAs monitoring.

Classical methods that include oligonucleotide microarrays, Northern blotting and real-time polymerase chain reaction are frequently-used for miRNAs assay, but they commonly encounter the limitations of the complex and expensive equipment, cumbersome primer designs and operational steps, high cost and throughput [5,6]. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technology with excellent accuracy, simple operation processes and low-cost devices, has emerged as a pivotal technique in trace detection as well as miRNAs assay. ECL biosensors consist of biological recognition elements and signal transducers [7]. As the core components of transducers, luminophores have evolved from the initial tris(bipyridine)ruthenium [8] and luminol [9] to diverse ECL structures such as quantum dots [10], nanoclusters [11] and nano carbon materials [12,13] etc. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have boomed in ECL biosensors owing to the virtues of favorable photophysical, electrochemical properties, unique band gap structure [14,15]. Nevertheless, a common phenomenon widely named as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect has appeared in the field of solid-state CDs derived from their excessive resonance energy transfer or strong π-π stacking between adjacent layers tends to weaken or quench the ECL emission [16,17]. Some effective methods have been used to address this issue, such as hexadecylamine, poly(ethylene glycol), and polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane have been used to prepare functionalized CDs [18]. Moreover, surfactants, as the good improver, also have been employed to enhance the water solubility of hydrophobic organic molecules and suppress the ACQ effect [19]. Inspired by the above advantages, surfactants may be excellent candidates to suppress the ACQ effect of CDs. Considering the low abundance of miRNAs, catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) and strand displacement reaction (SDR) are used as nonenzymatic signal amplification strategies in nucleic acid signal transduction and amplification reactions to help achieve effective monitoring [20].

Herein, we proposed three kinds of CDs-based nanocomposites which prepared from citric acid and urea via solvothermal method functionalized by different surfactants (nonionic, cationic and anionic surfactant). Among them, CDs-based nanocomposites functionalized by nonionic surfactant of Triton X-100 (T-CDs) not only suppress the ACQ problem, but also perform the highest cathodic ECL emission. Therefore, utilizing T-CDs as a luminophore, a "Signal on" ECL biosensing platform has been established for accurate detection of miRNA-21 (as a template molecule) integrating with CHA and SDR strategies.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%) was provided by Kelong chemical Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), urea (99%) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were purchased from Mackin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, 99.99%), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99%), Triton X-100 (99%) and N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS, 98%) were acquired from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The DNA sequences used for miRNA-21 detection are listed as follows: the single strand DNA 1 (S1): 5′-TCT ACA CAT GGA CAT GGA GAT TTG GCA A-3′; S2: 5′-COOH-AAG CCA AAT CTC CAT GTC GGA CAA CCT TAT GGC-3′; the hairpin DNA 1 (H1): 5′-TCA ACA TCA GTC TGA TAA GCT ACC ATG TCC ATG TGT AGA TAG CTT ATC AGA CT-3′; H2: 5′-TGA TAA GCT ATC TAC ACA TGG ACA TGG TAG CTT ATC AGA AAA TC TCC ATG TCC ATG TGT AGA-3′. The high-purity water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was utilized in experimental studies. Other reagents and apparatus are shown in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) and different CDs-based nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared by a bottom-up strategies [21]. Firstly, 2 mmol CA and 4 mmol urea were added to 30 mL of toluene. The mixture was transferred into an autoclave lined with Teflon steel, which was heated at 210 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the solvents were removed by rotary evaporation to obtain CDs.

10 mg of CDs was dissolved in an appropriate amount of tetrahydrofuran, and then the mixture was dropped into an aqueous solution containing 310 μmol·L−1 of Triton X-100 (non-ionic surfactant) under intense stirring, kept stirring for 0.5 h, dialyzed, and freeze-dried to obtain Triton X-100 functionalized CDs-based nanocomposites (T-CDs). Meanwhile, SDS (anionic surfactant) and CTAB (cationic surfactant) were also used to functionalized prepare with similar steps (denoted as S-CDs and C-CDs) as controls ( Scheme 1).

Before subsequent operations, each hairpin was annealed at 95 °C for 5 min and cooled down to room temperature. 10 μL of 5 μmol·L−1 H1, 10 μL of 5 μmol·L−1 H2 and 10 μL of miRNA-21 were mixed into a centrifuge tube with 20 μL of Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) buffer (pH 7.4), and the mixture was reacted at 37 °C for 1 h to form H1-H2 duplex (H1-H2).

Initially, 10 mg of T-CDs was dispersed in 3 mL of water. Then, 2 mL of chitosan solution (CS, 0.5%) was added and stirred continuously for 0.5 h (Fig. S10), so as to provide abundant amino groups for the introduction of carboxy-modified S2. Then, 10 μL of the above solution (0.2 mg·mL−1) was dropped onto pre-treated clean GCE (T-CDs/GCE) [22] and dried at room temperature. Meanwhile, 2 μL of S1 (100 μmol·L−1) and S2 (100 μmol·L−1) were added into 300 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to obtain S1-S2 duplex (S1-S2) through hybridization. Then, 96 μL of EDC/NHS (4:1) were blended and reacted for 0.5 h to activate the carboxyl of S2. Next, 10 μL of S1-S2 was dropped on T-CDs/GCE and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h to get S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE via amide bond. The remaining active sites were sealed by 3 μL of 1 wt% bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 1 h (BSA/S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE). Subsequently, 10 μL of toluidine blue (TB) (100 μmol·L−1) was added dropwise and incubated for 15 min (TB/BSA/S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE). Finally, 10 μL of H1-H2 was incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h to complete the chain displacement reaction (H1-H2/TB/BSA/S1-S2/T-CDs/GCE). The modified electrode was rinsed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) to remove the physical adsorption after each step.

Fig. 1A-D shows the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of CDs and different CDs-based nanocomposites. CDs are well dispersed with an average diameter about 2 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CDs exhibits lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, implying the presence of (100) lattice planes of graphene (inset of Fig. 1A) [23]. The different CDs-based nanocomposites show diverse morphologies. A still dispersed state is appeared in S-CDs with an average diameter of 3.36 nm (Fig. 1B), uneven shapes and sizes aggregates are gotten in C-CDs with an average length of 74.1 nm (Fig. 1C), while the fusiform aggregates with an average length of 92.8 nm and a width of 31.2 nm are formed in T-CDs under the mediation of Triton X-100 (Fig. 1D). A clear boundary is presented on T-CDs with the blank area, indicating that Triton X-100 exhibits helping aggregation effect. Thus, the morphology and size of CDs-based nanocomposites are affected by the type of surfactant significantly.

The elemental compositions of different CDs-based nanocomposites were revealed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1E). It can be seen that the S-CDs exhibits a S2p peak corresponding to sulfur contained in the molecule of SDS (curve a). High-resolution XPS of S-CDs show graphite C at 284.5 eV, C-N at 285 eV, amide at 399.5 eV, amine at 400.0 eV and OC-O at 532.2 eV (Fig. S1A-C) [24]. The peak at 68.19 eV (curve b) is ascribed to Br –, and the N1s peak is mainly derived from amide (399.5 eV) and NH4 + (402.5 eV) (Fig. S1E). The results of chemical state analysis of T-CDs (Fig. S1G-I) supported by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1F d). The above results indicate that the sp2-carbon domain of CDs is functionalized by carbonyl, ester, amino and amide groups.

Fig. 1F displays three stretching vibration bands in all FTIR spectra, that is, the characteristic peaks of benzene ring carbon skeleton at 1599 cm−1, 1581 cm−1, 1550 cm−1, 1492 cm−1, aromatic ring unsaturated hydrocarbon at 3026 cm−1, and CN or CO bond at 1670 cm−1. The absorption bands (1240–1210 cm−1) are attributed to OSO3 - group (Fig. 1F b) [25]. A broad band at 3432 cm−1 is obtained on C-CDs assigned to the N-H stretching vibration, and the peaks appeared at 2918 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 arising from secondary amine group anti-stretching and C-H stretching respectively (Fig. 1F c) [26]. Fig. 1F d exhibits the peak gotten at 3470 cm−1 referring to the stretching vibration of O-H, the tensile vibration of C-H (at 2860 cm−1) and the intensity of characteristic peak of C-O-C (at 1100 cm−1) are also significantly higher than those in CDs owing to the content of C-H and C-O-C increased with the introduction of Triton X-100. These results demonstrate the successful combination of different surfactants with CDs.

The optical properties of CDs and different CDs-based nanocomposites were investigated via ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectrometer and fluorescence (FL) spectrophotometer. A typical π-π * transition absorption peak at 281 nm is observed in the UV–vis of CDs, and the n-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 system is seen at 407 nm (Fig. S2a) [27]. The homologous absorption peaks are also detected on S-CDs and C-CDs (Fig. S2b and c). While the obvious red-shifted main absorption peaks at 295 and 435 nm are emerged on T-CDs (Fig. S2d), suggesting the increased conjugation and narrowed optical band gap. At the same time, the peak emerged at 278 nm belongs to Triton X-100 [28]. The FL emission spectra of CDs and different CDs-based nanocomposites is observed at 556 nm (Ex = 450 nm) in aqueous medium (Fig. S3). The emission intensity increases after the functionalization of CDs with different surfactants, which thanks to the aggregation of CDs in the aqueous media has been prevented [29].

The water contact angle experiments were performed to monitor the difference in wetting state of different materials. The contact angle (θ) of CDs is 85.8° ( Fig. 2A), indicating a stronger hydrophobicity (Taking θ = 65.0° as a reference value [30]). The immoderate hydrophilicities are shown on S-CDs (θ = 19.8°, Fig. 2B) and C-CDs (θ = 41.1°, Fig. 2C) with the help of surfactants, while T-CDs (θ = 61.3°, Fig. 2D) presents moderate hydrophilicity [31]. During the ECL test, immoderate hydrophilicity is not conducive to a stable anchor of the material onto the surface of electrode.

Tauc plots and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were used to explore the energy levels of the different materials (Fig. S4A-C). The calculated band gap energies of CDs, S-CDs, C-CDs and T-CDs are decreased from 2.24 to 2.05 eV successively (Fig. 2E) [32], while the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels are determined to be - 2.49 eV, - 2.56 eV, - 2.64 eV and - 2.77 eV respectively (Table S2). The relatively lower-lying LUMO level and the smallest band energy gap between LUMO and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is gained from T-CDs, attributing to the high electronic affinity and transition to promote the excitation and transfer of electrons [33,34].

The ECL behaviors of CDs and different CDs-based nanocomposites were studied with K2S2O8 as a coreactant (50 mmol·L−1, pH 7.4) ( Fig. 3 A). Bare GCE presents a weak ECL intensity of 802 a.u. (curve a), and the increasing ECL intensities of about 1023, 2455, 4965 and 17275 a.u. are observed on CDs/GCE, S-CDs/GCE, C-CDs/GCE and T-CDs/GCE (curve b-e). Interestingly, the ECL intensity of T-CDs is 16.8 times that of CDs. Fig. 3B-D present the intensities of the S-CDs/S2O8 2−, C-CDs/S2O8 2− and T-CDs/S2O8 2− system under continuous scanning for 20 cycles, T-CDs/S2O8 2− system possesses the desired stability with the lowest relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.8%. The possible reason lies in the immoderate hydrophilicity of S-CDs and C-CDs make it difficult to anchor on the GCE surface during the ECL test, resulting in the lower stability [35]. This moderate hydrophilicity of T-CDs is beneficial for the stability of the interface. Besides, irreversible oxidation-reduction process is shown on S-CDs/GCE (Fig. S5). The smallest potential difference of the oxidation and reduction peak is acquired on T-CDs/GCE (0.20 V), suggesting the best redox reversibility [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Further discussion on the properties of T-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Triton X-100 is composed of poly(ethylene oxide) chain (hydrophilic part), phenyl ring and octyl group (hydrophobic part). The HRTEM images of T-CDs depict the crystalline part is surrounded by non-crystalline region (Fig. S6), hinting that hydrophobic CDs may be enclosed by self-enriched Triton X-100 due to π-π stacking between the aromatic structure of Triton X-100 and the CDs [37]. When CDs is surrounded by Triton X-100, the polar poly(ethylene oxide) chain of Triton X-100 is exposed to the periphery [28,38], the graphitizing cores in CDs with π-π interactions are kept within a certain distance through these polar long chains, resulting in the ACQ effect of CDs can be effectively suppressed and supported the emission of solid-state fluorescence. The solid phase absolute photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) of T-CDs and CDS are determined by integrating sphere measurements as 10.2% and 1.7%, respectively. Therefore, T-CDs with relatively higher PLQY, best ECL performance and good stability was selected as the luminophore for further experiments.

Fig. 4A depicts the 3D ECL spectrum of T-CDs/S2O8 2− system with a maximum emission wavelength at 555 nm. Meanwhile, the ECL spectrum of T-CDs and the FL emission spectrum overlap better (Fig. 4B), implying that T-CDs involves a non-surface state ECL reaction mechanism [39]. Fig. 4C shows the UV–vis absorption before and after the reduction of T-CDs by using hydrazine hydrate (See Section 4 of the Supplementary Material). It can be seen that the absorption peak belonging to the carbonyl group at 435 nm is significantly diminished, indicating that the carbonyl group is reduced. At this time, the ECL performance of T-CDs is also weakened (Fig. S7), proving the carbonyl group plays an important role in the ECL behavior of T-CDs [40].

Electrocatalytic electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was utilized to probe the generation and evolution of reactive radicals. As shown in Fig. 4D, the characteristic signal peaks of the DMPO-OH adduct (1:2:2:1 quartet pattern) and DMPO-SO4 adduct are observed in K2S2O8 and DMPO solution. However, the weak EPR intensity of DMPO-SO4 is obtained on account of the formation of •OH (hydroxyl radicals) between SO4 •− (radical ion) and H2O (curve a), as well as the self-scavenging effects of SO4 •− [41]. After the addition of T-CDs, the EPR intensity is significantly enhanced (curve b), demonstrating T-CDs has a positive impact on the production of SO4 •− and •OH. The defective edges and oxygen functional groups (carbonyl group) in the carbon framework are considered as the active sites to catalyze persulfate and promote the production of SO4 •− and •OH radicals [39,42]. These radicals further react with the T-CDs to generate more excited-state T-CDs* , resulting in a high ECL response.

The reasons for the good ECL performance of T-CDs are discussed as follows. (1) The energy gaps of T-CDs are smaller. The high electron affinity and transition make it easier for electrons on HOMO to be excited and transferred to LUMO to generate holes, which facilitates the rapid injection of radical into holes to produce ECL emission [43]. (2) The good stability of T-CDs in ECL test thanks to the appropriate hydrophilicity. (3) In the presence of K2S2O8, a large amount of generated intermediate SO4 •− could be improved by T-CDs. (4) The ACQ effect of CDs is effectively suppressed, the increased PLQY also exhibits a positive effect on ECL behavior [44].

The corresponding ECL mechanism of the T-CDs/S2O8 2− system is proposed. First, T-CDs•– and SO4 •– are produced by electrochemical reduction of T-CDs (Eq. 1) and S2O8 2– (Eq. 2), respectively. Second, T-CDs stimulates more SO4 •– generation (Eq. 3), and then T-CDs•– react with SO4 •– to produce abundant T-CDs* (Eq. 4). Finally, ECL emission occurs when T-CDs* decays from the excited state return to the ground state (Eq. 5).

Moreover, TB was utilized as a quencher for this sensing platform. The mechanism is discussed as follows. The ECL intensities of T-CDs/S2O8 2− system is significantly quenched by the addition of TB (Fig. S8A). EPR spectra show the significantly decrease of SO4 •− and OH• with the addition of TB to the T-CDs/ S2O8 2− system (Fig. S8B). This quenching effect is mainly achieved by consuming SO4 •− and OH• through the redox reaction with TB [45]. The obvious overlap between the ECL spectrum of T-CDs and the UV–vis spectrum of TB suggests the ECL energy transfer between T-CDs (donor) and TB (acceptor) (Fig. S8C) [46]. Further exploration of the flash photolysis dataset using the Stern-Volmer equation (eq. S6) to obtain decay time. The FL lifetime decay of T-CDs is 6.40 ns (Fig. 3I), whereas a reduction of FL lifetime that as the addition of TB (Fig. S9A and B), further evidencing the energy transfer. Thus, the ECL intensity of T-CDs was successfully quenched by the dual-quench strategy of ECL energy transfer and the principle of redox reaction of TB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility verification of the biosensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: S1 attaches to S2 through base-pairing to form S1-S2, which is anchored to the surface of T-CDs/GCE (Signal on) via the amide bond (Fig. S11). Next, the quencher (TB) can be intercalated into the S1-S2 double helix through π-π stack [47], the ECL intensity of T-CDs/S2O8 2− system is dual quenched by energy transfer from T-CDs to TB and the redox reaction of TB (Signal off). There is no spontaneous interaction between H1 and H2 in the absence of a target substance. With the addition of target miRNA-21, it hybridizes with the exposed toehold of H1 to unfold the hairpin structure. Then the exposed part of H1 hybridizes with the free end of H2 to form H1-H2, and the released miRNA-21 is able to initiate new CHA cycle. The predesigned S1-S2 preserves a toehold, H1-H2 can fully bind with S1 to induce SDR, and open the S1-S2 duplex to release the TB, resulting in the recovered ECL intensity (Signal on). Such favorable strategy offers a sensitive approach for quantitative analysis of miRNA-21 (Scheme 1B).

Different DNA samples were analyzed by 16% polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE), and the results are shown in Fig. 5 A. H1, H2, S1, S2 and miRNA-21 show different bands in Lanes 1, 2, 5, 6 and 9, respectively. Compared the distinct bands of Lanes 3 and 4, CHA reaction can only be induced in the presence of miRNA-21. The formation of S1-S2 is confirmed in Lane 7. A new high-brightness band is appeared at the top in Lane 8, indicating that H1-H2 (CHA products) is successfully displaced S2 and coupled with S1 to form H1-H2-S1 hybrids, and an imperceptible dim band is appeared at the bottom, corresponding to the released of S2, while the other bands are generated by the incomplete reaction. As expected, the feasibility of the strategy is verified.

The electrode modified process was characterized in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- via cyclic voltammetry (CV). From Fig. 5B, a distinct redox peak is appeared on bare GCE (curve a), and an obvious decreased peak current is observed after the addition of T-CDs (curve b), due to the impeditive effect of surfactant. After the modification of S1-S2 and BSA onto the T-CDs/GCE, further reduced redox currents (curves c and d) are observed due to the hindrance of biomolecules. As the incubation H1-H2 on the surface of above-modified electrode, a slight increased redox peak current is obtained (curve e). The results of EIS (Fig. S12) are consistent with the CV, confirming the successful preparation of the modified electrodes. In addition, Fig. S13 displays the modified process of C-CDs platform.

After the optimization of the experimental parameters (Fig. S14), the ECL performance of the system at gradient concentrations of target miRNA was measured via "Signal on" mode with T-CDs, C-CDs and S-CDs as luminophores. The ECL intensities increase with incremental concentration of miRNA-21, and exhibits dynamic ranges from 10 fmol·L−1 to 10 nmol·L−1 and from 100 fmol·L−1 to 10 nmol·L−1 for T-CDs/S2O8 2− system and C-CDs/S2O8 2− system, respectively ( Fig. 6A and B). The regression equations are: I (T-CDs) = 1635.14 lg c + 27203.52 (R
2 = 0.998) and I (C-CDs) = 508.92 lg c + 8608.20 (R
2 = 0.995), where I and c represent the ECL intensity and miRNA-21 concentration (Fig. 6C and D). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 2.9 fmol·L−1 and 22 fmol·L−1, respectively (S/N = 3). Unfortunately, the S-CDs/S2O8 2− system is not suitable for constructing the sensor (Fig. S15). The above results demonstrate that the T-CDs/S2O8 2− system owns superior analytical performance and a lower detection limit is basically due to the integration of the T-CDs as luminophore and the nucleic acid signal amplification strategy. The as-proposed biosensor based on T-CDs was further investigated. Although the sensor has the limitation of non-reusable in real-world applications, it is competitive with previous sensors (Table S3).

Stability, reproducibility and selectivity are crucial performances for the assessment of an ECL biosensor. The stable ECL responses are observed by consecutive scans for 21 cycles with RSD of 1.8% (10 fmol·L−1) and 1.6% (1 nmol·L−1), respectively ( Fig. 7 A). Repeatability was studied using five different electrodes under the same conditions, the RSD of obtained ECL intensity is 1.7% (Fig. 7B), revealing acceptable reproducibility (1 nmol·L−1 miRNA-21).

Some miRNAs containing miRNA-146a-5p, miRNA-24–3p, and miRNA-4652–5p (with the same concentration of 100 nmol·L−1) were employed as interferences to evaluate the selectivity of the constructed biosensor. The ECL signal of the biosensor with miRNA-21 (1 nmol·L−1) is much higher than that without target. When these interfering substances were analyzed separately, the ECL intensities are all very close to the blank solution. In addition, the approximate ECL intensity of the mixture (1 nmol·L−1 miRNA-21 mixed with 100 nmol·L−1 other three miRNAs) is obtained (Fig. 7 C). These results declare a high selectivity of this proposed method toward the target miRNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the biosensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of this proposed sensor in detecting miRNA-21 in real samples, first, recovery tests were executed using various concentrations of miRNA-21 spiked into diluted human serum (10%), the sample recoveries range from 97.1% to 109%, with the RSD value in the range of 1.9% to 3.5% (Table S4). Moreover, the cell lysates of breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells) and human epithelial cervix carcinoma cells (HeLa cells) were also studied. The ECL responses induced by the extract of MCF-7 cells is significantly stronger than that of HeLa cells at the same concentration, signifying that miRNA-21 expression is higher in MCF-7 cells than in HeLa cells (Fig. 7D). This is consistent with previous reported results [48]. Such results indicate that the proposed biosensor has a potential application prospect in practical detection.

In summary, three novel ECL CDs-based nanocomposites were prepared through the modulation of different surfactants. Among them, T-CDs possessed the strongest ECL emission was used to fabricate a "Signal on" biosensor, which achieved ultrasensitive monitoring miRNA-21 at a LOD of 2.9 fmol·L−1 (S/N = 3) with the help of a CHA and SDR amplification. The reason for the good results lies in the following factors. On the one hand, the polar long chain of Triton X-100 kept the graphitizing cores in CDs with π-π interactions within a certain distance, effectively suppressing the ACQ effect and obtaining a relatively narrow band gap. On the other hand, T-CDs have moderate hydrophilicity and can promote K2S2O8 to produce more SO4 •− to further generate excited state for ECL. Moreover, the proposed ECL sensor integrating T-CDs and nucleic acid amplification strategy afforded an accurate assay of miRNA-21. Finally, since CHA and SDR can be customized for different target sequences, the proposed strategy can be extended to detect different miRNA to achieve early diagnosis purposes in clinical applications.

Yuqin Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing-original draft. Gui Zhang: Conceptualization, Visualization. Li Song: Validation. Guangrong Kuang: Software. Yilan Ding: Data curation. Shuchun Bu: Data curation. Yaqin Chai: Formal analysis, Resources. Yingzi Fu: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22077105).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135314.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuqin Yang is a M.S. candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Gui Zhang received his M.S. degree in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Li Song is a Ph.D. candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Guangrong Kuang received her M.S. degree in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Yilan Ding is a MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.
Shuchun Bu is a MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwest University, China.

Yaqin Chai is a professor of chemistry in Southwest University, China. She received a Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Southwest Normal University (China) in 2004. Her main research interests are the bioelectrochemical sensors and electrochemical sensors.

Yingzi Fu is a professor of chemistry in Southwest University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Southwest University in 2006. Her research focuses on chemical sensors and biosensors.
